nativeRange.habitat: Wide variety of reef environments, especially lagoons and reef slopes; forms encrusting plates and fan-like foliose structures

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Supplemental feeding of small zooplankton, baby brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, oyster eggs, cyclops, and enriched planktonic foods one to two times per week has been reported to support growth. Polyps tend to extend mainly at night, so target feeding in the evening after lights out is generally recommended. Calcium, strontium, and trace elements should be maintained through regular dosing or supplementation.

avoidWith: corals placed within sweeper tentacle range (~10 cm)

summary: Merulina ampliata is an LPS reef coral with ruffled, fan-like foliose plates and meandering ridges. Generally considered intermediate-level care; requires moderate-to-high lighting, medium flow, and stable reef chemistry.
careNotes:
Merulina ampliata (Ridge Coral / Lettuce Coral) is an LPS stony coral in the family Merulinidae notable for its ruffled, fan-like or subarborescent colony form with meandering valleys radiating from center. Colonies can grow encrusting plates up to 1 m across with short gnarled branches in shallower, higher-flow conditions.

Key care requirements:
• Lighting: Moderate to high intensity advised (approximately 75–200 PAR for typical middle placement). Some sources suggest higher PAR (300–450+) when placed toward the upper tank; acclimate new specimens gradually by starting lower and moving upward over 2–4 weeks. Browning indicates insufficient light; bleaching indicates excessive light.
• Flow: Medium water movement generally recommended. Sufficient flow to prevent detritus settling on the colony surface is important given the coral's plate-like structure and irregular topography.
• Placement: Middle of the aquarium is commonly advised for standard reef conditions. Middle-to-upper positioning has also been described when higher lighting is available.
• Aggression: This coral is regarded as aggressive due to its sweeper tentacles, which extend primarily after lights out. Sources report sweeper reach of approximately 7–20 cm (a wide spread — maintain at least 10–15 cm clearance from neighboring corals to be cautious). Any coral downstream or adjacent may be stung after dark.
• Feeding: Polyps are rarely visible during the day; they tend to extend at night. Target-feeding of zooplankton, baby brine shrimp, mysis, oyster eggs, or cyclops in the evening is commonly recommended, approximately once or twice weekly. Flow should be reduced or turned off during feeding to allow polyp contact with food particles.
• Water chemistry: Stable alkalinity (8–12 dKH), calcium (380–430 ppm), and magnesium (approximately 1250–1350 ppm) are advised. The species does not tolerate significant parameter swings well.
• Fragging: Colonies that have developed branching structures can be fragged by cutting 2-inch or longer branches and mounting on frag plugs. Encrusting-phase specimens cannot be fragged easily until branching has begun.
• Growth form variability: Growth habit ranges from flat encrusting plates to foliose fans to short branching columns, often combining multiple forms in a single colony. This morphological flexibility is characteristic of the species.

Sources noted disagreement on sweeper tentacle range (LiveAquaria: ~7.6 cm / 3 inches vs. FreshMarine: ~15–20 cm / 6–8 inches); 10 cm is used as the aggressionRangeCm field value. Maintain conservative spacing in practice.

Sources also disagreed on temperature: LiveAquaria gives 22–26°C, Reeflex gives 25–29°C. The 23–27°C range adopted here represents the cross-source average.
breedingNotes: Fragmentation is the primary propagation method in captivity. Colonies that have developed branching projections can be fragged by cutting sections of at least 5 cm and mounting on frag plugs or rubble. Encrusting-phase colonies must develop branching before fragging is practical. Sexual reproduction via broadcast spawning occurs on natural reefs but is not practiced in the hobby.

additional.name: Frag Box Corals – Merulina Coral
additional.notes: Cross-checked care level (Difficult), lighting (High), flow (Medium), placement (Middle to Top), compatibility (Aggressive), growth rate (Moderately Fast). Confirmed sweeper tentacle aggression and nocturnal polyp extension.

additional.name: FreshMarine – Merulina Coral
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (74–84°F / 23–29°C), pH (7.8–8.4), salinity (1.019–1.024), native range (Indian Ocean, Pacific Ocean, Red Sea). Sweeper tentacle range listed as 6–8 inches (15–20 cm), which disagrees with LiveAquaria's ~3 inches (7.6 cm). Salinity lower bound (1.019) appears atypically low; LiveAquaria's 1.023 adopted as conservative minimum.

additional.name: Reeflex – Merulina ampliata
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (24.9–29°C), adult size (20–100 cm), minimum tank (~150L), depth range (0–40 m), native range (Red Sea, Africa, Indo-Pacific). Confirmed zooxanthellate, nocturnal polyps.

additional.name: WoRMS – Merulina ampliata taxonomy
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy confirmation (Order: Scleractinia, Family: Merulinidae) and native range (Indo-Pacific including Indonesia, Philippines, Australia, Red Sea). Colony size reported exceeding 1 m across and 0.5 m tall.
